news_summary: Recent health advancements reveal a breakthrough pregnancy vaccine significantly cutting infant RSV hospitalizations, new insights into immune cells boosting exercise endurance, innovative quantum computing aiding cancer drug development, and discoveries of gene activity differences in male and female brain cells.
news_summary_fr: Les récentes avancées dans le domaine de la santé mettent en lumière un vaccin révolutionnaire contre la grossesse qui réduit considérablement les hospitalisations de nourrissons dues au VRS, de nouvelles découvertes sur les cellules immunitaires qui améliorent l'endurance physique, l'informatique quantique innovante au service du développement de médicaments anticancéreux, ainsi que des différences dans l'activité génétique des cellules cérébrales chez les hommes et les femmes.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances en el ámbito de la salud revelan una vacuna revolucionaria contra el embarazo que reduce significativamente las hospitalizaciones infantiles por VSR, nuevos hallazgos sobre las células inmunitarias que mejoran la resistencia al ejercicio, la informática cuántica innovadora que contribuye al desarrollo de fármacos contra el cáncer y descubrimientos sobre las diferencias en la actividad genética de las células cerebrales masculinas y femeninas.

articles.title: Pregnancy vaccine reduces baby hospital admissions for RSV by 80%
articles.summary: A study confirms the vaccine gives excellent protection for babies against life-threatening chest infections.
articles.content: This new study shows the protection is nearly 85% when given at least four weeks before baby is born. Some protection is still possible if the jab is given later than this.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical breakthrough where a pregnancy vaccine reduces hospital admissions for babies due to RSV by 80-85%, indicating a major positive impact on infant health and potentially saving many lives.
articles.personality_title: Pregnancy vaccine cuts baby hospital stays from RSV by 80%
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) is a common virus that can cause serious chest infections in babies. These infections often lead to hospital stays, especially for very young infants.

**What happened** – A new study found that giving a vaccine to pregnant women can protect their babies from severe RSV infections. The vaccine works best when given at least four weeks before the baby is born, reducing hospital admissions by nearly 85%. Even if the vaccine is given later in pregnancy, it still offers some protection.

**Impact** – This vaccine is important because it helps keep babies healthy during their first months, a time when they are most vulnerable to RSV. By lowering the number of babies needing hospital care, the vaccine can reduce stress on families and hospitals.

**What's next step** – Health experts may recommend this vaccine for pregnant women to protect newborns. Further research could explore how to make the vaccine even more effective and available to more people.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A vaccine given during pregnancy can greatly reduce serious RSV infections and hospital stays for newborn babies.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un vaccin pendant la grossesse réduit de 80 % les hospitalisations des bébés dues au VRS
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le virus respiratoire syncytial (VRS) est un virus courant qui peut provoquer de graves infections pulmonaires chez les bébés. Ces infections entraînent souvent des hospitalisations, surtout chez les nourrissons très jeunes.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une nouvelle étude a montré qu’un vaccin donné aux femmes enceintes peut protéger leurs bébés contre les infections sévères au VRS. Le vaccin est le plus efficace lorsqu’il est administré au moins quatre semaines avant la naissance, réduisant les hospitalisations de près de 85 %. Même s’il est donné plus tard, le vaccin offre encore une certaine protection.

**Impact** – Ce vaccin est important car il aide à garder les bébés en bonne santé pendant leurs premiers mois, période où ils sont les plus vulnérables au VRS. En réduisant le nombre d’hospitalisations, il diminue aussi la pression sur les familles et les hôpitaux.

**Prochaine étape** – Les experts de la santé pourraient recommander ce vaccin aux femmes enceintes pour protéger les nouveau-nés. D’autres recherches pourraient améliorer son efficacité et son accès.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un vaccin administré pendant la grossesse peut fortement réduire les infections graves au VRS et les hospitalisations des nouveau-nés.
articles.personality_title_es: Vacuna en el embarazo reduce en 80 % las hospitalizaciones por VRS en bebés
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El virus respiratorio sincitial (VRS) es un virus común que puede causar infecciones graves en el pecho de los bebés. Estas infecciones a menudo requieren hospitalización, especialmente en bebés muy pequeños.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo estudio encontró que vacunar a mujeres embarazadas puede proteger a sus bebés contra infecciones graves por VRS. La vacuna funciona mejor cuando se aplica al menos cuatro semanas antes del nacimiento, reduciendo las hospitalizaciones en casi un 85 %. Incluso si se aplica más tarde, la vacuna sigue ofreciendo cierta protección.

**Impacto** – Esta vacuna es importante porque ayuda a mantener sanos a los bebés durante sus primeros meses, cuando son más vulnerables al VRS. Al reducir las hospitalizaciones, también disminuye la carga para las familias y los hospitales.

**Próximo paso** – Los expertos en salud podrían recomendar esta vacuna a las mujeres embarazadas para proteger a los recién nacidos. Nuevas investigaciones podrían buscar hacer la vacuna aún más eficaz y accesible.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una vacuna dada durante el embarazo puede reducir significativamente las infecciones graves por VRS y las hospitalizaciones en recién nacidos.
articles.image_prompt: A gentle, glowing pregnant silhouette cradling a softly illuminated shield shaped like a vaccine vial, surrounded by delicate, protective bubbles that encase a peaceful, sleeping baby nestled safely inside, all rendered in warm, natural earth tones with soft brushstrokes.

articles.title: Immune cells have a surprising role in exercise endurance
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 17 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01245-wStudy in mice suggests that B cells help regulate muscle performance.
articles.content:
B cells, an important component of the immune system, might improve endurance and muscle strength.Credit: Hinterhaus Productions/Getty

Immune cells that help to fight off foreign invaders in the body also provide crucial support for muscles during exercise, suggests a mouse study published in Cell today1.

B cells are the ‘security guards’ of the immune system, identifying harmful pathogens and actively producing antibodies to target them, says Peng Jiang, an immunologist at Tsinghua University in Beijing and anthor of the study. But until now, the cells’ role in metabolism had not been observed. Jiang says this is the first time that a function for these cells that is not associated with the immune system has been described— “a finding completely beyond our initial expectations”, he says.

Carolin Daniel, the director of the Helmholtz Munich Institute for Metabolism and Immunology in Germany, says that there is increasing interest in studying the functions of B and T cells beyond the immune system. The finding that B cells can be crucial intermediaries between the immune system and the organs involved in exercise is an important conceptual advance, she adds.

Run, run, run

Jiang’s team studied whether there was a connection between the immune system and exercise performance. They tested the endurance of mice that had been genetically modified to have a low B-cell count by having them run on a treadmill. The researchers increased the speed at predetermined intervals over a period of about 15 minutes, stopping when the mouse became exhausted.

They repeated the experiment with another group of mice that they treated with an antibody therapy, which is used in humans to target cancer-causing B cells. The antibodies destroy any B cells that they come across.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a novel scientific discovery showing that B cells, known for immune defense, also play a crucial role in regulating muscle endurance and performance. This breakthrough expands understanding of immune system functions beyond infection control and could lead to new therapies enhancing exercise capacity and metabolic health, benefiting broad populations.
articles.personality_title: Immune cells help muscles work better during exercise, study finds
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – B cells are a type of immune cell known for fighting infections by producing antibodies. Until now, scientists believed their main job was only to protect the body from germs.

**What happened** – Researchers at Tsinghua University in China studied mice with fewer B cells and tested how long they could run on a treadmill before getting tired. They found that mice with fewer B cells had less endurance. The team also used a treatment that removed B cells and saw similar results.

**Impact** – This study shows that B cells do more than fight infections; they also help muscles perform better during exercise. This is the first time scientists have discovered a role for B cells outside the immune system, connecting them to muscle strength and endurance.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to learn how B cells support muscles and whether this can help people improve their fitness or recover from muscle problems. This discovery might lead to new treatments that boost exercise ability and overall health.

**One-sentence takeaway** – B cells, immune cells known for fighting germs, also help muscles work better and improve exercise endurance.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les cellules immunitaires aident les muscles à mieux fonctionner pendant l'exercice, selon une étude
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les cellules B sont un type de cellule immunitaire connues pour combattre les infections en produisant des anticorps. Jusqu'à présent, on pensait que leur rôle principal était uniquement de protéger le corps contre les germes.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'université Tsinghua en Chine ont étudié des souris avec moins de cellules B et ont testé combien de temps elles pouvaient courir sur un tapis roulant avant de se fatiguer. Ils ont découvert que les souris avec moins de cellules B avaient moins d'endurance. L'équipe a aussi utilisé un traitement pour éliminer les cellules B et a observé les mêmes résultats.

**Impact** – Cette étude montre que les cellules B font plus que combattre les infections ; elles aident aussi les muscles à mieux fonctionner pendant l'exercice. C'est la première fois que les scientifiques découvrent un rôle des cellules B en dehors du système immunitaire, en lien avec la force et l'endurance musculaire.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent comprendre comment les cellules B soutiennent les muscles et si cela peut aider les gens à améliorer leur forme ou à récupérer de problèmes musculaires. Cette découverte pourrait mener à de nouveaux traitements pour augmenter la capacité à faire de l'exercice et la santé en général.

**En résumé** – Les cellules B, connues pour combattre les germes, aident aussi les muscles à mieux fonctionner et à améliorer l'endurance à l'exercice.
articles.personality_title_es: Las células inmunitarias ayudan a que los músculos funcionen mejor durante el ejercicio, según un estudio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las células B son un tipo de célula del sistema inmunitario conocidas por combatir infecciones produciendo anticuerpos. Hasta ahora, se creía que su función principal era solo proteger al cuerpo de gérmenes.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad Tsinghua en China estudiaron ratones con menos células B y probaron cuánto tiempo podían correr en una cinta antes de cansarse. Descubrieron que los ratones con menos células B tenían menos resistencia. El equipo también usó un tratamiento para eliminar células B y vio resultados similares.

**Impacto** – Este estudio muestra que las células B hacen más que combatir infecciones; también ayudan a los músculos a funcionar mejor durante el ejercicio. Es la primera vez que los científicos encuentran un papel para estas células fuera del sistema inmunitario, conectándolas con la fuerza y resistencia muscular.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren entender cómo las células B apoyan a los músculos y si esto puede ayudar a las personas a mejorar su condición física o recuperarse de problemas musculares. Este descubrimiento podría conducir a nuevos tratamientos para aumentar la capacidad de ejercicio y la salud en general.

**Resumen en una frase** – Las células B, conocidas por combatir gérmenes, también ayudan a los músculos a funcionar mejor y a mejorar la resistencia durante el ejercicio.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a strong, glowing mouse silhouette running energetically on a softly textured treadmill track, surrounded by gentle, radiant clusters of stylized B cell shapes resembling protective, shimmering shields that subtly intertwine with muscle fibers beneath the surface, symbolizing the immune cells’ supportive role in exercise endurance.

articles.title: Quantum computers take on health care: light-sensitive cancer drugs win US$2-million contest
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 16 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01236-xQuantum machines are making inroads into biology but have no ‘advantage’ over classical machines yet.
articles.content:
The winner of the Q4Bio US$2-million prize used the IBM Quantum System One computer, which is housed in the Cleveland Clinic, to do simulations.Credit: Ryan Lavine for IBM

A team developing light-sensitive cancer drugs has won a US$2-million Quantum for Bio (Q4Bio) prize for demonstrating a potential use for quantum computers in health care. But no team was awarded the competition’s $5-million grand prize.

Prize organizers announced the winning team, made up of researchers from quantum software firm Algorithmiq, based in Helsinki, technology giant IBM and the Cleveland Clinic hospital in Ohio, on 16 April.

The winning group is working on improving a type of cancer drug that can be activated using light once it reaches a tumour, making it less toxic to the rest of the body than regular therapies are. The team members simulated the way in which one such drug molecule interacts with light. They eventually want to work out how tweaking the make-up of the active molecule in the drug affects its properties, with the hope of applying it to many cancer types.

Such complex simulations are too difficult for today’s quantum computers to perform. Instead, the team used a hybrid technique, with computational chemistry methods for parts of the simulation and regular computers to process inputs and outputs, says Sabrina Maniscalco, a quantum physicist and the chief executive and co-founder of Algorithmiq.

Hard problems

The hardest parts of the simulation — replicating the way in which photons interact with electrons in a molecule — were performed on a nascent quantum computer at the Cleveland Clinic, she says. “It’s exactly the type of problem quantum computers are good at simulating,” she adds.

These kinds of simulation can still be done using classical methods. However, the team won the US$2-million prize for demonstrating that the same algorithms, when run on more-capable quantum systems in the future, should be able to glean information about the molecules that would be impossible using classical simulations, Maniscalco says. The algorithms could also be applied to other molecular problems, such as the design of new antimicrobial drugs, she adds.

Quantum computers are already tackling problems in physics and chemistry, with some researchers claiming to have performed calculations that would be impossible on classical machines, a capability called ‘quantum advantage’. But applications in biology have remained elusive.

Although it was feasible for a team participating in the Q4Bio competition to achieve quantum advantage, that was not achieved — nor was it the explicit aim of the competition.

Instead, research teams were challenged to develop algorithms and ways of working so that applications in biology would be ready once the hardware is in place, says Jonathan Hirst, a computational chemist at the University of Nottingham, UK. Hirst co-leads one of the finalist projects, which aims to improve a drug to treat the genetic disorder myotonic dystrophy.

High bar

The contest opened in 2023 with the backing of US-based Wellcome Leap, a non-profit organization based in San Diego, California, that was set up by UK charity the Wellcome Trust.

When the competition started, “no one had taken the bold bet to connect” quantum computing and biology, says Shihan Sajeed, Q4Bio’s programme director. Dozens of entrants were whittled down to six in three stages, with finalists receiving up to $4.25 million in research funding across the duration of the 30-month challenge.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant advancement in using quantum computing to improve cancer drug design, demonstrating a promising real-world application that could lead to less toxic therapies and broader medical benefits. The impact is meaningful for public health and the research is well-detailed, showing progress in a challenging interdisciplinary field.
articles.personality_title: Quantum computers help design light-activated cancer drugs, winning $2 million prize
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Quantum computers are powerful machines that can solve certain problems faster than regular computers. Scientists have hoped to use them to study biology and medicine, but so far, they have not shown clear advantages in this area.

**What happened** – A team of researchers from Algorithmiq, IBM, and the Cleveland Clinic won a $2-million prize for using an IBM quantum computer to simulate how light-sensitive cancer drugs work. These drugs can be turned on by light only at the tumor site, which could reduce side effects. The team used a mix of quantum and regular computing to study how the drug molecules interact with light.

**Impact** – This work is important because it shows how quantum computers might help design better cancer treatments in the future. Simulating molecules that interact with light is very hard for today’s computers, but quantum machines can handle parts of these problems. The methods developed could also help create new medicines for other diseases.

**What's next step** – The team plans to improve their simulations and explore how changing the drug molecules affects their behavior. As quantum computers get stronger, these methods could lead to new cancer drugs that are safer and more effective. Other researchers are also working on similar projects for different diseases.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Researchers used quantum computing to simulate light-activated cancer drugs, winning a $2-million prize and paving the way for better treatments in the future.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les ordinateurs quantiques aident à concevoir des médicaments contre le cancer activés par la lumière, remportant un prix de 2 millions de dollars
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les ordinateurs quantiques sont des machines puissantes capables de résoudre certains problèmes plus rapidement que les ordinateurs classiques. Les scientifiques espèrent les utiliser en biologie et en médecine, mais jusqu’à présent, ces machines n’ont pas montré d’avantages clairs dans ces domaines.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de chercheurs d’Algorithmiq, IBM et du Cleveland Clinic a remporté un prix de 2 millions de dollars pour avoir utilisé un ordinateur quantique IBM afin de simuler le fonctionnement de médicaments contre le cancer sensibles à la lumière. Ces médicaments peuvent être activés uniquement au niveau de la tumeur, ce qui pourrait réduire les effets secondaires. L’équipe a utilisé un mélange d’informatique quantique et classique pour étudier l’interaction des molécules du médicament avec la lumière.

**Impact** – Ce travail est important car il montre comment les ordinateurs quantiques pourraient aider à concevoir de meilleurs traitements contre le cancer à l’avenir. Simuler les molécules qui interagissent avec la lumière est très difficile pour les ordinateurs actuels, mais les machines quantiques peuvent gérer certaines parties de ces problèmes. Les méthodes développées pourraient aussi aider à créer de nouveaux médicaments pour d’autres maladies.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe prévoit d’améliorer ses simulations et d’étudier comment modifier les molécules du médicament influence leur comportement. À mesure que les ordinateurs quantiques deviendront plus puissants, ces méthodes pourraient conduire à de nouveaux médicaments contre le cancer plus sûrs et plus efficaces. D’autres chercheurs travaillent aussi sur des projets similaires pour différentes maladies.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des chercheurs ont utilisé l’informatique quantique pour simuler des médicaments contre le cancer activés par la lumière, remportant un prix de 2 millions de dollars et ouvrant la voie à de meilleurs traitements à l’avenir.
articles.personality_title_es: Computadoras cuánticas ayudan a diseñar medicamentos contra el cáncer activados por luz y ganan premio de 2 millones de dólares
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las computadoras cuánticas son máquinas potentes que pueden resolver ciertos problemas más rápido que las computadoras normales. Los científicos esperan usarlas en biología y medicina, pero hasta ahora no han mostrado ventajas claras en estas áreas.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de investigadores de Algorithmiq, IBM y la Clínica Cleveland ganó un premio de 2 millones de dólares por usar una computadora cuántica IBM para simular cómo funcionan los medicamentos contra el cáncer sensibles a la luz. Estos medicamentos se activan solo en el tumor, lo que podría reducir los efectos secundarios. El equipo usó una combinación de computación cuántica y clásica para estudiar cómo las moléculas del medicamento interactúan con la luz.

**Impacto** – Este trabajo es importante porque muestra cómo las computadoras cuánticas podrían ayudar a diseñar mejores tratamientos contra el cáncer en el futuro. Simular moléculas que interactúan con la luz es muy difícil para las computadoras actuales, pero las máquinas cuánticas pueden manejar partes de estos problemas. Los métodos desarrollados también podrían ayudar a crear nuevos medicamentos para otras enfermedades.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo planea mejorar sus simulaciones y explorar cómo cambiar las moléculas del medicamento afecta su comportamiento. A medida que las computadoras cuánticas se vuelvan más potentes, estos métodos podrían llevar a nuevos medicamentos contra el cáncer que sean más seguros y efectivos. Otros investigadores también trabajan en proyectos similares para diferentes enfermedades.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Investigadores usaron computación cuántica para simular medicamentos contra el cáncer activados por luz, ganando un premio de 2 millones de dólares y abriendo camino a mejores tratamientos en el futuro.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing molecular structure illuminated by soft beams of light, surrounded by abstract representations of quantum circuits and gentle waves symbolizing photon-electron interactions, set against a calm, muted background evoking a research laboratory atmosphere.

articles.title: Revealed: how male and female brain cells differ in gene activity
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 16 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01227-yVariations in gene expression could help to explain why brain-disease risks differ according to sex.
articles.content:
Researchers found sex differences in the activity of genes within cortical neurons (pictured) and other brain cells.Credit: David Scharf/Science Photo Library

By analysing more than a million brain cells, researchers have uncovered widespread differences in patterns of gene activity between male and female brains.

The work, which defined sex on the basis of a person’s combination of sex chromosomes, could help to explain why the risk of developing some brain conditions — such as schizophrenia and Alzheimer’s disease — differs between males and females.

First atlas of brain organization shows development over a lifetime

Although the differences were subtle, the team identified more than 100 genes that showed consistent variation in their expression between males and females across several brain regions. The work was published on 16 April in Science1.

“Having these gene-expression signatures provides a molecular handle to understanding the biology of how the brains of men and women might be functioning slightly differently in the context of the different hormonal environments that their bodies produce,” says Jessica Tollkuhn, a neuroscientist and molecular biologist at Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory in New York. She adds that “understanding sex differences in disease susceptibility could lead to better treatments to benefit everyone”.

Subtle differences

Previous studies2,3 have documented sex differences when it comes to a person’s risk of developing various neurological conditions. For example, schizophrenia, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and Parkinson’s disease are more common in biological males — who typically have XY sex chromosomes. By contrast, Alzheimer’s disease and mood disorders such as depression and anxiety tend to be more common in females, whose sex chromosomes are usually XX.

“What underlies that has been a central question,” says Tollkuhn. Sex differences in the brain tend to be “extremely subtle”, she explains. “Most of the brain doesn’t show sex differences in its day-to-day function.”

However, molecular-level differences in the expression of genes between male and female brain cells could “modulate the impact of disease variants”, says study co-author Alex DeCasien, a computational and evolutionary biologist at the US National Institute of Mental Health in Bethesda, Maryland.

How fast your brain ages is affected by these 64 genes

To explore this, DeCasien and her colleagues studied cells from six regions of the brain’s cortex, identifying some 680,000 excitatory neurons and 290,000 inhibitory neurons, as well as 270,000 glial and other cells in tissue samples from 30 people.

In an analysis of more than 4,300 genes, the team reported that sex accounted for less than 1% of variation in gene expression across the brain cells. “This finding fits with what we already know about human variation — there is a lot more variation within a sex than between sexes,” says Donna Maney, a neuroscientist at Emory University in Atlanta, Georgia.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery revealing subtle but widespread gene expression differences between male and female brain cells. This research advances understanding of sex-based differences in brain disease risks, such as schizophrenia and Alzheimer's, which could lead to improved, more personalized treatments benefiting a broad population. The study is well-detailed and focused on a meaningful health-related breakthrough with potential large-scale impact.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find subtle gene activity differences in male and female brain cells
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long known that some brain diseases affect men and women differently. For example, conditions like schizophrenia and Parkinson’s disease are more common in men, while Alzheimer’s and depression often affect women more. But exactly why this happens has been unclear.

**What happened** – Researchers studied over a million brain cells from 30 people to look for differences in gene activity between male and female brains. They focused on cells from six areas of the brain’s cortex. They found more than 100 genes that showed small but consistent differences in activity between males (with XY chromosomes) and females (with XX chromosomes). This study was published on April 16, 2026, in the journal Science.

**Impact** – These subtle gene activity differences help explain why some brain diseases affect men and women differently. Understanding these differences at the molecular level could lead to better treatments tailored to each sex. For example, it might help scientists develop new medicines that work better for men or women based on how their brain cells behave.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue to explore how these gene activity differences influence the way brain diseases develop. They hope this knowledge will guide future studies and medical advances, potentially leading to more personalized and effective therapies.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New research shows small but important differences in gene activity between male and female brain cells, which could help explain why some brain diseases affect sexes differently and improve future treatments.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent de subtiles différences d’activité génétique entre cellules cérébrales masculines et féminines
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – On sait depuis longtemps que certaines maladies cérébrales touchent différemment les hommes et les femmes. Par exemple, la schizophrénie et la maladie de Parkinson sont plus fréquentes chez les hommes, tandis que la maladie d’Alzheimer et la dépression touchent davantage les femmes. Mais la raison exacte restait inconnue.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont étudié plus d’un million de cellules cérébrales provenant de 30 personnes pour chercher des différences d’activité génétique entre cerveaux masculins et féminins. Ils se sont concentrés sur des cellules de six régions du cortex cérébral. Ils ont trouvé plus de 100 gènes dont l’activité variait légèrement mais régulièrement entre hommes (chromosomes XY) et femmes (chromosomes XX). Cette étude a été publiée le 16 avril 2026 dans la revue Science.

**Impact** – Ces différences subtiles dans l’activité des gènes aident à comprendre pourquoi certaines maladies cérébrales affectent différemment les hommes et les femmes. Comprendre ces différences au niveau moléculaire pourrait permettre de développer des traitements mieux adaptés à chaque sexe. Par exemple, cela pourrait aider les scientifiques à créer des médicaments plus efficaces selon le fonctionnement des cellules cérébrales des hommes ou des femmes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à étudier comment ces différences d’activité génétique influencent le développement des maladies cérébrales. Ils espèrent que ces connaissances guideront de futures études et progrès médicaux, menant à des thérapies plus personnalisées et efficaces.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle recherche révèle de petites mais importantes différences d’activité génétique entre cellules cérébrales masculines et féminines, ce qui pourrait expliquer les différences dans l’impact des maladies cérébrales selon le sexe et améliorer les traitements futurs.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren sutiles diferencias en la actividad genética de células cerebrales masculinas y femeninas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Se sabe desde hace tiempo que algunas enfermedades cerebrales afectan de manera diferente a hombres y mujeres. Por ejemplo, la esquizofrenia y la enfermedad de Parkinson son más comunes en hombres, mientras que el Alzheimer y la depresión suelen afectar más a mujeres. Pero no se entendía bien por qué.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores analizaron más de un millón de células cerebrales de 30 personas para buscar diferencias en la actividad genética entre cerebros masculinos y femeninos. Se centraron en células de seis áreas del córtex cerebral. Encontraron más de 100 genes con diferencias pequeñas pero constantes en su actividad entre hombres (con cromosomas XY) y mujeres (con cromosomas XX). El estudio se publicó el 16 de abril de 2026 en la revista Science.

**Impacto** – Estas sutiles diferencias en la actividad genética ayudan a explicar por qué algunas enfermedades cerebrales afectan distinto a hombres y mujeres. Entender estas diferencias a nivel molecular podría ayudar a crear tratamientos más personalizados para cada sexo. Por ejemplo, podría facilitar el desarrollo de medicamentos que funcionen mejor según cómo se comportan las células cerebrales de hombres o mujeres.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán estudiando cómo estas diferencias en la actividad genética influyen en el desarrollo de enfermedades cerebrales. Esperan que esta información guíe futuras investigaciones y avances médicos, llevando a terapias más efectivas y adaptadas.

**Frase clave** – Una nueva investigación muestra diferencias pequeñas pero importantes en la actividad genética entre células cerebrales masculinas y femeninas, lo que podría explicar las diferencias en enfermedades cerebrales y mejorar los tratamientos futuros.
